title: OPPOSITION: Embrace 36 (003183541)
text:
OPPOSITION Nо B 3 183 541

Empatica S.r.l., Via Enrico Stendhal, 36, 20144 Milan, Italy (opponent), represented by Dragotti & Associati S.p.A., Via Nino Bixio 7, 20129 Milan, Italy (professional representative) 

a g a i n s t

Biotronik SE & Co. KG, Woermannkehre 1, 12359 Berlin, Germany (applicant), represented by Hildebrandt. Rechtsanwälte PartG mbB, Kurfürstendamm 72, 10709 Berlin, Germany (professional representative).

On 19/03/2026, the Opposition Division takes the following

DECISION:

REASONS

On 21/11/2022, the opponent filed an opposition against all the goods and services of European Union trade mark application No 18 726 391  (figurative mark). The opposition is based on the international trade mark registration designating the European Union No 1 318 930, ‘EMBRACE’ (word mark). The opponent invoked Article 8(1)(b) EUTMR.


LIKELIHOOD OF CONFUSION — ARTICLE 8(1)(b) EUTMR

Pursuant to Article 8(1)(b) EUTMR, a likelihood of confusion exists if there is a risk that the public might believe that the goods or services in question, under the assumption that they bear the marks in question, come from the same undertaking or, as the case may be, from economically linked undertakings. Whether a likelihood of confusion exists depends on the appreciation in a global assessment of several factors, which are interdependent. These factors include the similarity of the signs, the similarity of the goods and services, the distinctiveness of the earlier mark, the distinctive and dominant elements of the conflicting signs, and the relevant public.

a) The goods and services

The goods and services on which the opposition is based are the following:

Class 9: Mobile software application for use in connection with operating portable medical devices; mobile software application for collecting and tracking data associated with the physical, physiological and autonomic activity, temperature, stress, and sleep of an individual, and issuing electronic or telephonic alerts concerning same to patients, healthcare providers and caregivers.

Class 10: Portable medical devices with sensors to monitor and respond to the physical, physiological and autonomic activity, temperature, stress, and sleep of a person wearing or carrying the device.

Class 44: Medical monitoring, assistance and alert services provided via telecommunication and global computer networks for individuals with health problems through the use of wearable medical devices paired with a mobile software application.

The contested goods and services are the following:

Class 9: Mobile apps; animated films; motion picture films; computer application software for mobile phones; downloadable media; downloadable educational media; downloadable multimedia files; data processing equipment; software; data storage programs; computer software for collection, editing, organizing, modifying, book marking, transmission, storage and sharing of data and information; computer application software for mobile phones, namely, software for use in mobile apps, animated films, motion picture films, data storage, data editing, data organizing, data modifying, data transmission, data sharing; recordable and downloadable media; data processing equipment, programs and software.

Class 41: Providing a website featuring non-downloadable publications in the nature of books, magazines, brochures, pamphlets in the fields of treatment of neurological diseases; online electronic newsletters delivered by e-mail in the field of neurological diseases (not downloadable).

Class 44: Medical services, namely, services for patients and information relating to the medical field, namely, in the field of neurostimulation and pain management; informational services in the field of medical health, health care and neurology.

An interpretation of the wording of the goods or services is required to determine the scope of protection of these goods or services. 

In this respect, the General Court has confirmed that the term ‘namely’, used to show the relationship of individual goods or services to a broader category, is exclusive and restricts the scope of protection only to the goods or services specifically listed (04/10/2016, T‑549/14, Castello / Castelló et al., EU:T:2016:594, § 71; 31/01/2024, T‑581/22, ECE QUALITY OF LIFE (fig.) / ECE (fig.), EU:T:2024:47, § 43). 

As a preliminary remark, it is to be noted that according to Article 33(7) EUTMR, goods or services are not regarded as being similar to or dissimilar from each other on the ground that they appear in the same or different classes under the Nice Classification.

The relevant factors relating to the comparison of the goods or services include, inter alia, their nature, their intended purpose, their method of use and whether they are in competition with each other or are complementary (‘the Canon criteria’). It is also necessary to take into account, besides the Canon criteria, other factors, namely distribution channels, the relevant public and the usual origin of the goods or services (02/06/2021, T‑177/20, Hispano Suiza / Hispano Suiza, EU:T:2021:312, § 21-22).

Contested goods in Class 9

 
The contested mobile apps; computer application software for mobile phones; software; computer software for collection, editing, organizing, modifying, book marking, transmission, storage and sharing of data and information; computer application software for mobile phones, namely, software for use in mobile apps, animated films, motion picture films, data storage, data editing, data organizing, data modifying, data transmission, data sharing; data processing programs; data processing software in Class 9 overlap or share several similarity factors with the opponent's mobile software application for collecting and tracking data associated with the physical, physiological and autonomic activity, temperature, stress, and sleep of an individual, and issuing electronic or telephonic alerts concerning same to patients, healthcare providers and caregivers in Class 9. Although these contested goods are more general and may serve various purposes, whereas the opponent’s goods are narrowly defined and specialised, all of them are strongly reliant on software. Therefore, the Opposition Division considers these goods to be identical, or at least highly similar, because they coincide in nature, they might share the same methods of use, distribution channels, relevant public and providers. 

    
The contested downloadable media; downloadable educational media; downloadable multimedia files; recordable and downloadable media in Class 9 are similar to the opponent's mobile software application for collecting and tracking data associated with the physical, physiological and autonomic activity, temperature, stress, and sleep of an individual, and issuing electronic or telephonic alerts concerning same to patients, healthcare providers and caregivers in Class 9 because they might coincide in distribution channels, relevant public and producer. Moreover, they may be complementary, as such media can be accessed, downloaded or used through a mobile application, such as the one covered in the opponent’s list. 

The contested data processing equipment in Class 9 are similar to the opponent's mobile software application for collecting and tracking data associated with the physical, physiological and autonomic activity, temperature, stress, and sleep of an individual, and issuing electronic or telephonic alerts concerning same to patients, healthcare providers and caregivers in Class 9 because they are complementary in a sense that the opponent’s mobile software application specified above requires data processing in order to function properly, given that its purpose is to collect, analyse and process medically relevant data. Furthermore, the goods coincide in their relevant public and may originate from the same producers.    

The contested data storage programs in Class 9 are similar to the opponent's mobile software application for collecting and tracking data associated with the physical, physiological and autonomic activity, temperature, stress, and sleep of an individual, and issuing electronic or telephonic alerts concerning same to patients, healthcare providers and caregivers in Class 9 because they share the same nature and are complementary as the opponent’s mobile software application specified above requires storage of data program in order to collect and process them. Moreover, these goods may have the same purpose, which is to collect and retain relevant data. They might also originate from the same producers.    

The contested animated films; motion picture films in Class 9 and the opponent's goods or services do not have the same nature, purpose or methods of use and they rather do not target the same relevant public or share the same distribution channels. Furthermore, they are not complementary to each other or in competition and they are not usually produced or provided by the same undertakings. The opponent’s goods and services consist of software, devices or assistance related to the collection and processing of medically relevant data, whereas animated films and motion pictures films are generally intended for entertainment purposes and generally are not likely to have any connection with healthcare-related devices or services. Therefore, they should be considered dissimilar.  

Contested services in Class 41

The contested providing a website featuring non-downloadable publications in the nature of books, magazines, brochures, pamphlets in the fields of treatment of neurological diseases; online electronic newsletters delivered by e-mail in the field of neurological diseases (not downloadable) are similar to the opponent's Mobile software application for collecting and tracking data associated with the physical, physiological and autonomic activity, temperature, stress, and sleep of an individual, and issuing electronic or telephonic alerts concerning same to patients, healthcare providers and caregivers in Class 9 because they might share the same purpose, namely providing health-related information. The also coincide in the relevant public and provider.

Contested services in Class 44

The contested medical services, namely, services for patients and information relating to the medical field, namely, in the field of neurostimulation and pain management; informational services in the field of medical health, health care and neurology in Class 44 overlap with the opponent's medical monitoring, assistance and alert services provided via telecommunication and global computer networks for individuals with health problems through the use of wearable medical devices paired with a mobile software application in Class 44. Therefore, they are identical, or at least highly similar, because they have the same nature, purpose (which is to provide information about health), distribution channels, relevant public and providers. 

b) Relevant public — degree of attention

The average consumer of the category of products concerned is deemed to be reasonably well informed and reasonably observant and circumspect. It should also be borne in mind that the average consumer’s degree of attention is likely to vary according to the category of goods or services in question (22/06/1999, C-342/97, Lloyd Schuhfabrik, EU:C:1999:323, § 26). 

In the present case, the goods and services found to be identical or similar are mostly specialised goods and services related to healthcare that target medical professionals with specific professional knowledge or expertise, as well as patients.

The degree of attention for the goods and services in question, including specialised technological goods and services related to health, but also goods of general use, such as mobile apps or downloadable media, may vary from average to high.  

c) The signs

The relevant territory is the European Union.

The global appreciation of the visual, aural or conceptual similarity of the marks in question must be based on the overall impression given by the marks, bearing in mind, in particular, their distinctive and dominant components (11/11/1997, C‑251/95, Sabèl, EU:C:1997:528, § 23).

The unitary character of the European Union trade mark means that an earlier European Union trade mark can be relied on in opposition proceedings against any application for registration of a European Union trade mark that would adversely affect the protection of the first mark, even if only in relation to the perception of consumers in part of the European Union (18/09/2008, C‑514/06 P, ARMAFOAM / NOMAFOAM, EU:C:2008:511, § 57). This applies by analogy to international registrations designating the European Union. Therefore, a likelihood of confusion for only part of the relevant public of the European Union is sufficient to reject the contested application.

The earlier mark is a word mark, while the contested mark is a figurative mark. However, the stylisation of the contested mark is very basic and weak at best, as the word element ‘Embrace’ appears is a classic font and the number ‘0’ features only a simple decorative element resembling a vertical white line running through it.

For a substantial part of the relevant public, for example the German-, Italian- or Polish-speaking part of the public, the word element ‘EMBRACE’ present in both signs is meaningless and therefore, normally distinctive for the goods and services in question. The Opposition Division decided to focus on this part of the relevant public. 

Regarding the element ‘360’ of the contested mark, it cannot be excluded that this number will be associated to the number of degrees comprising a complete circle, or the number of degrees in one full rotation. In view of this semantic content, it will be perceived as a reference to a complete, holistic service that covers all the all aspects of the customers’ needs (05/07/2024, B 3 204 070, ANDROMEDA 360 / ANDROMEDA; 23/06/2022, B 3 146 092, INSIGHTS360 / 360 FUND INSIGHT). In this case it may be especially associated with a software or device tracking complex parameters of human condition or with holistic healthcare services. In consequence, it is non-distinctive for the goods and services at issue. 

The contested sign has no element that could be considered clearly more dominant than other elements.

Visually, the signs coincide in the word element ‘EMBRACE’, which is distinctive for the relevant public in relation to the goods and services in question. The signs differ in the additional element of the contested sign, namely the number ‘360’. However, this element is non-distinctive. The signs further differ in the contested sign’s figurative aspects, which are weak at best.

Therefore, the signs are visually similar to a high degree.

Aurally, irrespective of the different pronunciation rules in different parts of the relevant territory, the pronunciation of the signs coincides in the sound of the word ‘EMBRACE’, present identically in both signs, which is distinctive. 

It cannot be excluded that the element ‘360’ of the contested sign will not be pronounced, since consumers do not tend to pronounce non-distinctive verbal elements (30/11/2011, T-477/10, SE© Sports Equipment, EU:T:2011:707, § 55; 04/02/2013, T‑159/11, WALICHNOWY MARKO (fig.) / MAR-KO, EU:T:2013:56, § 44). Moreover, consumers naturally tend to shorten long marks in order to reduce them to the elements that they find easiest to refer to and remember (28/09/2016, T‑539/15, SILICIUM ORGANIQUE G5 LLR-G5 (fig.) / Silicium Organique G5- Glycan 5-Si-Glycan-5-Si-G5 et al., EU:T:2016:571, § 56).

Therefore, the signs are aurally similar to a high degree, if not identical. 

Conceptually, reference is made to the previous assertions concerning the semantic content conveyed by the marks. While the element ‘EMBRACE’ present in both signs is meaningless for the relevant part of the public, the element ‘360’ of the contested sign evokes the idea of completeness and integrity. To that extent, the marks are conceptually not similar. However, this conceptual difference is of very limited relevance in the overall comparison of signs, as it stems from a non-distinctive meaning.

Therefore, the signs are conceptually dissimilar, but the conceptual differences are of very limited relevance for the overall comparison. 

d) Distinctiveness of the earlier mark

The distinctiveness of the earlier mark is one of the factors to be taken into account in the global assessment of likelihood of confusion.

According to the opponent, the earlier mark has ‘medium/high level of distinctiveness’. In its observations it states that “Empatica is a ‘digital healthcare company" that builds and develops a digital platform considered among the most reliable and respected in the world, to improve human health’. Moreover, the opponent claims that the product EmbracePlus ‘is a milestone in remote health monitoring and physiological data collection for research studies and clinical trials of any scale. Awarded a research grant to support NASA’s Translational Research Institute for Space Health, it will address the protection of human health and performance across long duration, deep space exploration missions.’

To support his claim, the opponent submitted the following evidence:

	Annex 1: Printout of a scientific article written by four medical scientists titled: Seizure detection at home: Do devices on the market match the needs of people living with epilepsy and their caregivers? published in 2020 in the magazine Epilepsia, in English (23 pages). In the article there is one section dedicated to description of the characteristics of “Empatica Embrace” device. 

Irrespective of whether or not the opponent’s statements amount to an implicit claim of enhanced distinctiveness of the earlier mark, for reasons of procedural economy the opponent’s claim does not have to be assessed under Article 8(1)(b) EUTMR (see below in ‘Global Assessment’).

Consequently, the assessment of the distinctiveness of the earlier mark will rest on its distinctiveness per se. In the present case, the earlier trade mark as a whole has no meaning for any of the goods and services in question from the perspective of the public in the relevant territory. Therefore, the distinctiveness of the earlier mark must be seen as normal.

e) Global assessment, other arguments and conclusion

In the present case, the goods and services were found partly identical, partly similar, and some of them dissimilar. They target specialised public (medical professionals), as well as the public at large (patients, people taking care of their health), whose degree of attention may vary from average to high. The earlier mark has a normal degree of distinctiveness. The signs are visually similar to high degree, aurally similar to a high degree, if not identical, and conceptually not similar, although the conceptual differences have limited bearing on the overall assessment.

Likelihood of confusion covers situations where the consumer directly confuses the trade marks themselves, or where the consumer makes a connection between the conflicting signs and assumes that the goods covered are from the same or economically linked undertakings.

It is common practice nowadays for companies to make variations of their trade marks in order to name new lines of products, or to create a modernised version of them. Because the element ‘EMBRACE’ plays a distinctive role in both signs, the public under analysis will believe that both signs belong to the same ‘EMBRACE’ brand, whereas the element ‘360’ is an additional element meant to designate a specific line of products within the brand. In other words, it is highly conceivable that the public under analysis will perceive the signs as variations of the same mark, configured in different ways according to the type of goods that they designate (23/10/2002, T‑104/01, MISS FIFTIES (fig.) / Fifties, EU:T:2002:262, § 49).

Considering all the above, the Opposition Division finds that there is a likelihood of confusion at least but not necessarily only on the part of the public for whom ‘EMBRACE’ is meaningless, such as the German-, Italian- and Polish-speaking part of the public. Therefore, the opposition is partly well founded on the basis of the opponent’s international trade mark registration. As stated above in section c) of this decision, a likelihood of confusion for only part of the relevant public of the European Union is sufficient to reject the contested application.

It follows from the above that the contested trade mark must be rejected for the goods and services found to be identical or similar to those of the earlier trade mark.

The rest of the contested goods, namely animated films and motion picture films in Class 9 are dissimilar. As the identity or similarity of goods and services is a necessary condition for the application of Article 8(1)(b) EUTMR, the opposition based on this Article and directed at these goods cannot be successful.

Since the opposition is partially successful on the basis of the inherent distinctiveness of the earlier mark, there is no need to assess the enhanced degree of distinctiveness of the opponent's mark, as claimed by the opponent. The result would be the same even if the earlier mark enjoyed an enhanced degree of distinctiveness.

Likewise, there is no need to assess the claimed enhanced degree of distinctiveness of the opponent's mark in relation to dissimilar goods, as the similarity of goods and services is a sine qua non for there to be likelihood of confusion. The result would be the same even if the earlier mark enjoyed an enhanced degree of distinctiveness.

COSTS

According to Article 109(1) EUTMR, the losing party in opposition proceedings must bear the fees and costs incurred by the other party. According to Article 109(3) EUTMR, where each party succeeds on some heads and fails on others, or if reasons of equity so dictate, the Opposition Division will decide a different apportionment of costs.

Since the opposition is successful for only some of the contested goods and services, both parties have succeeded on some heads and failed on others. Consequently, each party has to bear its own costs.

The Opposition Division

 According to Article 67 EUTMR, any party adversely affected by this decision has a right to appeal against this decision. According to Article 68 EUTMR, notice of appeal must be filed in writing at the Office within two months of the date of notification of this decision. It must be filed in the language of the proceedings in which the decision subject to appeal was taken. Furthermore, a written statement of the grounds for appeal must be filed within four months of the same date. The notice of appeal will be deemed to have been filed only when the appeal fee of EUR 720 has been paid.
